column2: — I didn't tell you, Lilichka, — the grandmother spoke up.
— But Ivan did come by right away back then.
— Granny, I know.
— You know?
— Well, Mom told me.
— And why didn't she tell me that she told you?
Everyone here protected one another so much that troubling news was concealed, got tangled up, lost, and no one could remember whether anyone had told anyone anything or not.

So there were topics where someone might happen to say a name aloud, and the rest would freeze at the table, because they didn't quite remember whether they'd been trying to shield one of those present from unpleasant news.
Most often they'd glance at the grandmother, because protecting her was the most logical thing.
— Would it have changed anything?
— asked Lilichka.
— I was trying to protect the child.
She was only just getting used to it here back then.
— Where will I be in the autumn, grandmother?
— So are you going to that Australia or not?
— she asked and immediately went on: — Don't go!
— It's not like you to speak so unequivocally.
— And why don't you live at his mother's?
Could he find work there?
— At his mother's?
At Nati's?
The grandmother remembered how Greg had once come to them with Nati.
Nati talked all the time, despite the fact that in Spanish the grandmother knew the numbers, the names of the months and 'good day.'
They went to the market with Nati, and she was shouting something the whole time.
— Lilichka, do something with her, — the grandmother said then.
— Granny, what difference does it make to you.
Pretend you're not with her.
— Lilichka, look, she's already pawed all the tomatoes over there.
— Let her paw them.
Nati raked up tomatoes, peppers and kept saying something the whole time.
At home she indiscriminately kissed all the children, even the neighbors' ones who ran past the gate.
They were all her own to her.

So now too, hearing that the prospect of grabbing herself one more child had appeared, Nati kept pestering Greg and asking him to say something to Lilichka, that she was already ready to take Lusya in.
Having heard something about matches and Marta, she didn't want to listen any further.
But when Greg showed her a photo of Marta — dark-haired, tall, heavy-browed, on strong legs and with a big belly where twins were growing — Nati said: 'This one will be a good mother.
Greg, why do you choose women who are incapable of bearing you strong, dark-haired children?'

Looking at Lilichka's delicate shoulders, at the little blue veins that showed on her very thin neck, Nati immediately grasped the dim prospects of childbearing, though she did wonder why Lilichka's Marko, whom she loved more than her own blood grandson Roberto, had turned out such a strong, sinewy lad, who — and this was the main thing — needed no sunscreen at all, even though Lilichka insisted that he did.
Nati then looked at Marta and again repeated to Greg: 'My child, why don't you choose women who are reliable like this, like your own mother?'
— Mom, — Greg would say, — you do understand that she's not only dark-haired and sturdy in appearance like you, but her temperament is similar too.
— What are you saying?
It's good when a woman is talkative.
— How can I put it?
She's just as crazy as you are.
— So raise him like that, rock him in your arms, sing him songs, play him Mozart!
I played you Mozart, — Nati shouted, half-rising from the table.
— Back then Mozart was on cassettes, Greg!
On cassettes!
Cassettes were expensive!
And I played them!
Only for you to then come here with the grandchildren and tell me that your mother is crazy!
Such conversations could go on for hours, and it was for their sake, in fact, that Greg came.
And then, when he suddenly found himself with Lilichka, who always spoke quietly and was timid as a fawn, he couldn't get used to it.
— You shout, — Lilichka would tell him after Spain.
— And Marko shouts.
And Roberto.
You all shout.
It's unbearable.
Then she would take the car keys and spend an hour walking around a big out-of-town supermarket, carefully picking out yogurts.
— Grandmother, — Lilichka said now, looking at the grandmother's back as she walked slowly along the cut path.
— How could you think up such a thing?
To live with Nati!
— My girl, but was I ever clever?
There was no countering this classic line of the grandmother's.
— Granny, let's agree, until the end of summer we don't talk about Australia.
— And what are we to talk about, then?
— About anything at all.
— You've imposed an awful lot of censorship this summer.
I already feel eighty years younger — just like in my youth, conversations on forbidden topics with Maria in the kitchen.
And when Maria leaves?
Then who is there to talk to?
— What do you mean?
— Well, how...
— the grandmother turned around on the path, leaning on her cane.
Though it wasn't cold yet, she'd thrown a gray knitted sweater over her shoulders, which suited her gray hair terribly well.
— Don't talk about the father of Marta's children, don't talk about this craftsman of Miyechka's, don't talk about Ivan…
— Well, it's all clear here without words anyway.
— Oh, I know, Lilichka, it's clear that nothing is clear.
We'll talk about pumpkins and that orchard which I'm sick to death of, but Miyechka has decided to clear it, blast it.
— What's that orchard to you?
— What does she need it for?
She's got plenty to do without apples.
— Oh, she'll abandon it.
— Whom?
— The orchard.
— Ah.
— And who did you think of?
— That falls under the list of forbidden topics.
— Tell me.
— Well, what's there to say?
Did you see the way that non-believer craftsman looked at her?
— Forbidden topic!
Bee-e-e-eep!
Bee-e-eep!
— Lilichka imitated a beeping, threw herself into the bushes with a high leap, then jumped back onto the path and ran ahead.
— Your showing off told me more than I needed to know!
— the grandmother tossed at her back.
— Listen, Lilya, — Miyechka whispered in the dark, when the two of them had already lain down in their wide sisters' bed.
— Lilya, are you asleep?
— No, — Lilichka spoke up from the darkness.
— I'm not asleep.
— What if we aimed your idea about those hipsters at the apple orchard?
— What?
Which hipsters?
— Well, so that volunteers would come to clear the orchard and then harvest the crop together.
— I don't know.
— Ask someone.
— Who?
— Danylo?
— Oh, I'm not sure he's ever seen an apple on a tree.
— And do you know that grandmother knows about Ustym?
— How?
— He looked at you a lot, she says.
— And you gave yourself away right off, didn't you?
— I went bounding down the path like a madwoman.
— Lord.
— What?
— What did I do to deserve a sister like this?
— The one you've got…
— If you won't tell with words, then at the level of your body you've got these physical reflexes.
— So what.
We agreed anyway that this would be among the forbidden topics.
— If I had a different sister, I'd tell her everything.
— What different one?
— Well, exactly like you, only a bit more normal, — said Miyechka and immediately felt Lilichka's splayed toes already pinching her under the blanket.
By an old school habit Maria pretended to be asleep, while she herself listened to what problems were sweeping over her girls, so as not to miss anything important.
'And what if she were alone?
— she always thought.
— How would I ever find out what's in her head?'
The Terrace
And summer really did pass outside time and space.
And only rarely was someone in this house overcome by heavy thoughts.
The door had already been replaced.
The pumpkins were growing.
Now they were looking for workers to lay the terrace with new boards.
— Who needs that terrace?
— the grandmother kept repeating.
— The terrace is the most essential place in the house, — said Lilichka.
She was the most romantic of them all.
— Imagine, Granny, we'll lay a new terrace, set up an enormous table, all the husbands, children, ex-husbands, relatives will come, Nati will come… It'll be like it once was!
— You say it as if that could convince anyone of the necessity of a terrace, — said Marta.
She stood there in a shirt with silver cufflinks and her underwear.
— Do you know how much the grandmother once suffered over that?
— Martochka, — said the grandmother, — Martusya, you should put on some trousers.
— Nothing fits on me anymore, — said Marta.
— Well, buy yourself some trousers.
— I don't want to.
I'm fine in my underwear.
— But what if someone comes to work on the terrace?
— They'll survive.
It's summer outside.
— You've got shorts-style underwear.
Marta silently went into the house, went to the wardrobe and found a large elastic-waisted skirt, in which one of the girls had once danced some Gypsy dance.
The skirt was yellow, with large red polka dots.
Marta pulled it up under her belly, barely tugged on a black T-shirt with some princess on it over the top (on Marta's chest the princess was thrown askew) and went outside.
The grandmother, sitting on a chair in the middle of the yard, raised an eyebrow the way only Marta and she could.
— Still better than in your underwear, — the grandmother said calmly.
A bicycle pulled up to the gate.
An unfamiliar thin man climbed off the bicycle and walked toward them with an uncertain gait.
— Good health to you, — he said.
— And to you, — Marta spoke up, being the one standing closer.
— I've come to do the laying for you.
The grandmother came up to them.
— You'll be laying the boards?
— she said.
— I will, — answered the man, but there was no trusting him at all.
He wore tracksuit bottoms and a short-sleeved shirt buttoned up to the top.
A thin pale neck stuck out of the collar.
— You said there'd be a crew?
— asked Marta.
— It got fucked up, — said the man.
Marta glanced at the grandmother.
The grandmother thought for a moment, and then answered:
— I see.
— What do you see, Granny?
— said Marta, laughing.
The man came up to the terrace.
By the terrace lay stacked boards that had been delivered a week earlier, but could not be laid because there was no one to take up the work.
— We need screws, — said the man, looking at the boards with disgust.
Marta called Miyechka, and they drove to the market for screws.
— So when will the crew come?
— asked Miyechka, once they'd driven away from the yard.
— It got fucked up, — said Marta.
— What?
— It got fucked up.
That's how this gentleman explained it.
The gentleman meanwhile sat by the boards, his head resting on his knees.
Folded in half, he seemed almost inhumanly small.
His head was covered with thin fair hair.
On his face, if you looked closely, under his nose were ginger whiskers.
